data.title: Amazon's Breakout Is Here - Outsized AWS & Advertising Performance
data.description:
I previously covered Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN), (AMZN:CA) in August 2025, discussing how its ability to monetize the ongoing AI boom had been undisputed, as observed in AWS' richer LTM operating margins and the growing multi-year backlog.

Combined with the growing advertising opportunities arising from the growth flywheel across commerce, the Prime subscription/streaming, and the logistics capabilities, I had believed that the stock's wide moat remained highly compelling - resulting in my reiterated Buy rating upon a moderate dip then.

In this article, I shall discuss AMZN's recent outperformance in the FQ3'25 earnings call across the AWS and advertising segments, with it underscoring the company's highly successful monetization cadence beyond commerce.

Despite the break out post FQ3'25 earnings call, I am of the opinion that the stock remains a compelling Buy for those looking to tap into the multi-year cloud super cycle and the secular advertising growth in the streaming end markets.

Renewed Monetization Opportunities Beyond eCommerce

AMZN 1Y Stock Price

Since my last Buy article, AMZN has finally broken out of its sideways trading cadence since early January 2025, with the stock also charting new heights of $250s by early November 2025.

Much of its tailwinds are attributed to the sandbagged FQ3'25 guidance, the consequent double beat FQ3'25 performance, and the promising FQ4'25 guidance.

These underscore how AMZN has been able to drive profitable growth across the outsized AWS and advertising performance, with the impacted North American operating incomes merely attributed to one-time legal settlement with the Federal Trade Commission.

1. AWS/Trainium

AWS has delivered on its undisputed cloud leading position with 29% in market share in Q3'25, through the accelerated revenue growth to $33B in FQ3'25 (+6.8% QoQ/+20.2% YoY) despite the tougher YoY comparison from a year ago at +19% YoY - with it being "our largest growth rate in 11 quarters."

This is especially since Trainium2 has been seeing "strong adoption, is fully subscribed is now a multibillion-dollar business that grew 150% quarter-over-quarter," as AMZN previously "gone back to our manufacturing partners multiple times to produce much more than we’d originally planned.”

Despite AMZN previously offering Trainium2's "same computing power as Nvidia’s H100 chips at 25% of the price," it is apparent that the hyperscaler is still able to generate profitable cloud operations thus far, as observed in the richer LTM operating margins of 35.9% (+0.6 points sequentially/+9.7 from FY2019 levels of 26.2%).

These developments reinforce the fact that AWS remains AMZN's bottom-line driver while lending credence to the management's aggressive data center/custom AI accelerator investments thus far.

With Project Rainier coming online by October 29, 2025, I am of the opinion that AWS is likely to report outsized FQ4'25 performance as the company monetizes the growing capacity.

This is while delivering (part) of the growing multi-year remaining performance obligation of $200B in FQ3'25 (+2.5% QoQ/+21.9% YoY), with it lending great insights to AWS' long-term growth prospects, as the management also hints at outsized FQ4'25 backlog growth in the recent earnings call:

Backlog grew to $200 billion by Q3 quarter end and doesn't include several unannounced new deals in October, which together are more than our total deal volume for all of Q3. AWS is gaining momentum. (Seeking Alpha)

Combined with the projected doubling in AWS capacity by 2027, it goes without saying that AMZN may remain a beneficiary of the ongoing cloud boom.

2. Advertising

Moving on to the next growth driver, AMZN's advertising.

Despite the nascency compared to the commerce and AWS segment, I have observed an accelerating growth rate in its advertising revenues to +22% YoY (excluding FX) in FQ3'25, compared to a year ago at +19% YoY.

When compared to its direct peers, Google (GOOG) at +12.6% YoY/+10.4% YoY and Meta (META) at +25.5% YoY/+18.5% YoY, respectively, it is apparent that AMZN may eventually be a force to be reckoned with in the advertising segment while potentially challenging the current duopoly.

Growing CTV Advertising Opportunities

My optimism arises from AMZN Demand-Side Platform's [DSP] highly strategic partnerships with other CTV players, including The Walt Disney Company (DIS), Roku, Inc. (ROKU), and Netflix (NFLX).

This strategy has directly triggered AMZN's expanded exposure to the CTV advertising end market, since these four streaming platforms (including Prime Video) comprise 19.5% of the total TV viewing share and 43.1% of the total streaming share as of September 2025.

This is significantly aided by AMZN's doubled ad load on Prime video to "4 to 6 minutes of ads per hour, up from 2 to 3.5" in early 2024, with it implying the growing advertiser base and the higher demand for its ad inventory, thanks to Prime Video's robust mindshare where:

  • "47% of users stream at least 2–3 times per week,
  • 15% watch daily, and
  • 89% stream weekly or more."

With eMarketer already expecting CTV ad sales to grow from $28.79B in 2024 to $46.89B in 2028, expanding at a CAGR of +12.9%, and "98.4% of those dollars going to video ads, solidifying its place as one of the fastest-growing major ad channels in the US," I am of the opinion that AMZN's advertising monetization may remain robust moving forward.

Decelerating Growth Meets Reasonable Valuations

The Consensus Forward Estimates

As a result of the numerous growth levers across commerce, AWS, and advertising, amongst others, it is unsurprising that AMZN has already offered an excellent FQ4'25 revenue guidance of $209.5B at the midpoint (+16.2% QoQ/+11.5% YoY) and operating income guidance of $23.5B (+35% QoQ/+10.8% YoY).

These numbers do not appear overly aggressive as well, based on the YTD outperformance at +11.8% YoY and +32% YoY, respectively.

These reasons may also be why the consensus have been raising their forward estimates, with AMZN expected to report an accelerated top/bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +11.4%/+19.7% through FY2027, with it underscoring its profitable growth cadence.

This is despite the notable deceleration from the 5Y historical growth profile at +17.9%/+36.9% and the 9Y historical growth at +21.9%/+64.4% (the period that AMZN is profitable), respectively.

AMZN Valuations

As a result of the maturing growth profile and the consequently downgraded P/E valuations from the prior 5Y mean of 167.70x, AMZN has seemingly found a floor at the FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 33.34x, near to its 1Y mean of 33.87x.

Thanks to the moderation, AMZN remains rather reasonable at my estimated 3Y PEG non-GAAP ratio of 1.69x as well (based on the FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 33.34x and the the consensus 3Y adj EPS growth estimates of +19.7%), compared to the 5Y mean of 4.54x, the sector median of 1.64x, and its peers, including:

  • commerce/advertising - Sea Limited (SE) at 3Y PEG non-GAAP ratio of 0.55x (using the same methodology),
  • commerce/advertising - Mercado Libre (MELI) at 1.63x,
  • commerce/advertising - Shopify Inc. (SHOP) at 4.42x,
  • commerce/hyperscaler/advertising - Alibaba (BABA) at 2.41x,
  • hyperscaler/advertising - Microsoft (MSFT) at 1.78x,
  • hyperscaler/advertising - Alphabet (GOOG) at 1.58x, and
  • advertising - Meta (META) at 1.99x.

These developments underscore that AMZN is an even better buy after the moderation of the prior rally post FQ3'25 earnings call.

AMZN Remains A Great Buy

AMZN 5Y Stock Price

For now, AMZN has finally broken out of its prior sideways trading after the blowout FQ3'25 earnings call, with the stock charting new heights of $250s while running away from the 50/100/200 day moving averages.

Based on the LTM adj EPS performance at $6.72 (+43.8% sequentially) and the 1Y P/E mean of 33.87x, it is apparent that the stock is still trading near to my fair value price target of $227.60, based on the current stock prices of $234.60.

Based on the consensus raised FY2027 adj EPS estimates of $9.48, there remains an excellent upside potential of +36.7% to my long-term price target of $321 as well.

AMZN Technical Indicators

AMZN's technical indicators appear somewhat reasonable across the RSIs and the trading volumes, with the recent AI-related market-wide meltdown already triggering an improved margin of safety for those looking to add.

This is especially since the October 2025 breakout implies a potential bull-run similar to those observed in 2018, 2020, and 2023-2025, thanks to its robust fundamental performance across AWS cloud computing, Advertising, and commerce, amongst others.

As a result of the multi-pronged growth drivers, I am reiterating my buy rating for the AMZN stock.

Based on the stock's ongoing retest of the second support lines of $230s, interested readers may want to observe the price movement for a little longer before adding, since a further pullback to the established base support line/200 day moving averages of $215s may trigger an expanded long-term return profile.

Given the potential -8.3% downside from current levels, some patience may be more prudent in the mean time.

data.title: IREN Vs. Cipher: The Former Looks Less Promising
data.description:
Introduction

I noticed that comparison articles are becoming more popular, especially now, when we're witnessing a new germination of AI-enablers and, at the same time, a major shift in old business models. Every company is trying to establish itself in the new hyper-high-growth niches within the aggregate AI addressable market, so it's interesting to pick wisely among multiple emerging alternatives. That's why I decided to compare IREN Limited (IREN) with Cipher Mining (CIFR). Both were mainly Bitcoin (BTC) miners before, but they're now rapidly transitioning toward neocloud and data‑center capacity operators. They have their own distinctions in terms of operating activities, but it's interesting to compare their business model shifts so that we can pick the best-in-class miner-to-AI converter for the long haul.

My View On IREN Limited

IREN initially started as a renewable‑powered Bitcoin miner, and they still make over 96% of sales from mining (as of September 2025), but when the AI started to make moves in recent years, they decided to use their renewable energy advantage and existing racks to capitalize on the new theme. Their sites in British Columbia and Texas are powered 100% by clean or renewable energy, and they own/control land with substations and complete data‑center infrastructure. They run a vertically integrated system, so they have more control over costs, and they can theoretically repurpose capacity away from mining toward AI relatively smoothly.

Back in January 2025, the management guided in its update that the mining segment's longer‑term targets 57 EH/s by H2 2025, which should have been supported by a 750 MW build‑out at Childress in Texas and fleet upgrades with Bitmain S21 Pro and S21 XP miners, targeting sub‑15 J/TH efficiency and Childress power costs around 2.8c/kWh on a net basis. Then, in March 2025, they secured ~2.75GW for data centers built in West Texas with a new 600 MW grid connection agreement, and then later in the same month, IREN stated that it would "pause all further Bitcoin Mining expansion upon completion of its 52 EH/s buildout."

By mid-April, they reached 40 EH/s, then raised additional capital "to fund the cost of entering into the prepaid forward transaction" in June 2025. They finally reached the mid-year target of 50 EH/s by the end of June, and started to expand their AI Cloud with thousands of new Nvidia Blackwell GPUs again and again, doubling capacity to 23k GPUs by the end of September. Then the management signed a new multi-year AI cloud contract with a customer, who was willing to secure demand for 11k of their 23k GPUs, which was going to bring in ~$250 million in AI Cloud ARR by the year-end. In mid-October, they priced another convertible notes offering, raised $1 billion at 0% (42.5% conversion premium, due 2031), and then secured $9.7 billion AI Cloud contract with Microsoft (MSFT) in early November.

IREN is moving extremely fast in its business transition, and there has been so much corporate news in just a few months, staying on track to deploy 140k GPUs by the end of 2026 and reach $3.4 billion in AI Cloud ARR (they had only 1,896 NVIDIA H100/H200 GPUs in early 2025 for comparison). By Q1 2026, the plan is to reach $500 in ARR, and then move from that point up to $3.4 billion, which is effectively a 6.8x expansion in less than 12 months.

The deal with MSFT is viewed by market participants differently, depending on their particular take on what comes next for the AI story. For some skeptics, the fact that IREN will have to spend about $8.8 billion in CAPEX to deliver 200 MW of AI compute in exchange for ~$10 billion of revenue over 5 years looks economically unfounded. Even if they reach 85% in EBITDA margin, the ROIC is going to be close to zero or only slightly positive. As one of X's users calculated, there will be a capital shortfall of over $2 billion that will need to be filled with additional debt or equity, and given that they were quite active in debt financing this year, the odds for equity-based financing, and hence dilution, are pretty high. On the other hand, the capacity they're building is essential to get as much of the future market share as possible, and even if they earn 0 in real profits from that MSFT deal, the scale built out over the next few years isn't going anywhere.

As the company jumps from a ~2 MW air‑cooled cluster to a 200 MW liquid‑cooled GB300 deployment over the next years, the execution risks are going to rise, but we should think of the opportunity that will likely come along with that extensive expansion. In growth stories like this, the stock price of an underlying company usually follows the revenue trajectory, and that's why the massive upticks in revenue revisions in recent months are outweighing the risks (at least for now).

My View On Cipher Mining

Cipher Mining is also an ex-pure-play Bitcoin miner, but it's progressing toward a power‑rich digital infrastructure developer to date. They initially went public through a SPAC structure to secure cheap power in West Texas, build large-scale and modular data centers further translate that into low all‑in BTC production costs, which is almost the path of IREN back in the day.

Cipher acquired and developed several Texas sites (Odessa, Black Pearl/Wink, plus JV sites like Alborz, Bear, Chief), energizing ~477 MW and ramping self‑mining capacity to ~23.6 EH/s, which put it in the top tier of global miners. But then the management realized that grid‑connected power is the real value AI needs, they began their strategic pivot to AI/HPC. The main thing for that pivot is Barber Lake in Texas, which is a 300 MW+ site with a new substation and permits already in place.

Their hosting capacity is in great demand from large hyperscalers right now. A few weeks ago, they signed Fluidstick and Google (GOOG) under the new 10-year agreement, which is going to bring in at least $3 billion (up to $7 billion) in contracted revenues for Cipher's 168 MW capacity. The economics here are very different from Cipher's legacy business: rather than volatile BTC revenue tied to price and difficulty, this is recurring streams, which gives CIFR better earnings visibility. What's also noteworthy - Google even took a 5.4% stake in CIFR as part of the deal. After Google, Amazon chose Cipher for powering some of its AI workloads through a 15-year lease agreement.

This quarter, we executed a pivotal transaction with Fluidstack and Google, which firmly established our credibility in the HPC space... we've now taken another major step forward... we've executed a second landmark HPC transaction, this time with Amazon Web Services.

Source: Seeking Alpha Earnings Call Insights

Effectively, CIFR's contracted AI hosting capacity went up from 0 in Q2 2025 to 544 MW as of Q3.

Taken together, the Google/Fluidstack and AWS contracts give Cipher at least ~$8.5 billion of long‑dated and mostly fixed‑rent revenue backlog (before Google's possible extensions) on 468 MW of AI/HPC capacity, all scheduled to start layering in from late 2026. But they need to finance this build-out somehow, which is why we see them attracting new notes offering (although at less favorable terms if we compare the issuance characteristics to IREN's recent issuance).

The business model run by Cipher differs from IREN's because the former is more of a "landlord" than a GPU cloud platform provider. The core HPC model is about developing data‑center infrastructure, then leasing that capacity (power, space, cooling, interconnect) on long‑term contracts, whereas IREN is buying and owning the GPUs and selling compute to its customers. So, IREN's revenue line is tied to GPU capacity and usage, not just rent on four walls and megawatts.

The deals CIFR signed recently, and the backlog they got is set to kick in from late 2026. Today, CIFR's profit and loss statement still looks mining‑centric because, as of Q3, they made ~$72 million in revenue (+64.6% YoY) with 629 BTC mined and 1,500 BTC held. I liked to see them narrowing the GAAP loss rapidly - in the latest quarter, we can see only a $3 million loss compared to a negative $41 million in adjusted loss last year. And the management has no plans to convert profitable sites like Odessa to HPC until at least 2027, which may be reasonable, as this approach protects cash flow generation (at least to some extent).

As they bring in new HPC DCs to live, their revenue is set to rise dramatically over the next few years, according to the current consensus. The revenue consensus revisions look similar to what we saw in IREN's case:

And I don't doubt that their top line is likely to outpace these projections amid new prospective contracts and the current contracts' capacity extensions. The return on the capital they invest isn't at risk to the extent I described it for IREN, because of the business models' difference. Cipher's CAPEX is basically land with substations, high‑voltage lines, shells, cooling plants, and campus infrastructure, which is combined a 20-30 year assets with clear residual value. That value isn't depreciating as fast as IREN's AI cloud assets, like GPUs. So, that's why I think that CIFR's economic IRRs will likely be materially better than what IREN can get out of its Microsoft project.

IREN Vs. Cipher - Valuation

The valuation side looks very interesting. CIFR and IREN are basically identically valued on the FWD P/E basis, but IREN is twice cheaper compared to CIFR in terms of the FWD P/S ratio:

As I've already noted above, Cipher's landlord-like business model is structurally designed to generate decent infrastructure‑style returns on long‑lived assets, whereas IREN's recent deals intentionally traded away project‑level returns to buy credibility and scale in GPU cloud. But IREN competes with already well-established players like CoreWeave (CRWV) and fast-growing Nebius (NBIS), so I assume that they're going to lose on margins because of the pricing pressure going forward, especially given that they've paused developing mining operations. That's why we see the consensus foreseeing deterioration on the bottom line by 2029 (it's likely to come earlier than that, in my view):

For CIFR, we don't have clear visibility because of the lack of investment coverage from big banks, but based on what we have, in FY2026, the EPS should grow by over 44% YoY:

This is where CIFR becomes cheaper than IREN: On the 2-year forward basis, CIFR is valued at less than 30x earnings for 44% YoY EPS growth, while IREN is valued at 38x for 9.54% YoY EPS expansion. I think that if both stocks trade at a PEG of 1.5x next year (close to the sector's norms), then IREN would have a large downside of over 62%, while CIFR will have an upside of over 120%.

Summary Thesis

I believe that the fact that Cipher shouldn't see GPU price swings and a lot of the tech obsolescence risk ahead that IREN is explicitly taking onto its own balance sheet, the market should be willing to overpay for it in terms of forward valuation. In other words, the lower hardware risks that CIFR holds should theoretically give it a higher valuation multiple ahead. Yes, CIFR and IREN aren't the same in the way they're trying to capitalize on the ongoing AI transition, but in terms of risk/reward, CIFR stands out to me, and that's why I'm rating it a "Buy", while rating IREN as a "Hold".

Good luck with your investments!

data.title: GRNJ: First Look At Tom Lee's New Small/Mid Cap Granny Shots ETF
data.description:
Introducing The Fundstrat Granny Shots US Small & Mid Cap ETF

Credit to Tom Lee - he's striking while the iron's hot. Tuesday marked the first day of trading for the Tidal Trust III - Fundstrat Granny Shots US Small- & Mid-Cap ETF (GRNJ), which was presumably created to capitalize on the incredible success of the $3.6B Fundstrat Granny Shots US Large Cap ETF (GRNY), which I reviewed last month. I was excited to get the email announcing the launch of trading of this new fund in a category I feel is underserved, so I loaded GRNJ's 52 holdings into my ETF database, made some calculations and comparisons, and now I'd like to present to you what I believe GRNJ does well and why it might earn a spot in your portfolio. I hope you enjoy the read, and as always, I look forward to the discussion afterward.

Overview

The Granny Shots Strategy

According to its website, GRNJ's investment process

"selects a portfolio of small- and mid-cap stocks aligned with key themes identified by the Portfolio Managers. This approach integrates a top-down fundamental research methodology with a quantitative, bottom-up stock selection strategy. Stocks that align with at least two of the identified themes are included in the Fund, with positions equal-weighted and rebalanced quarterly."

That last part caught my eye. Unlike GRNY, which reconstitutes monthly, GRNJ's portfolio changes quarterly, so I'm assuming this will result in less portfolio turnover. Still, the basic philosophy appears to be the same. Based on the graphic below, GRNY holds 29 stocks based, in part, on their style tilts, and 23 based, in part, on PMI Recovery. According to this section of the Granny Shots website, the strategy

"identifies a relationship between yield-curve dynamics that implies the latter has bottomed out, and seeks to gain exposure to companies benefitting from the forecasted PMI recovery."

Those are short-term themes, but GRNJ also holds stocks based on long-term themes covering energy and cybersecurity, millennials, global labor suppliers, and easing financial conditions. I believe stocks in these sections are more likely to be longer-term holds, but time will tell if that assumption is correct.

The above image is slightly outdated as of August 14, 2025, but it's worth reiterating that all stocks are initially equal-weighted. Put differently, companies satisfying four themes, like BWX Technologies (BWXT), won't receive higher weights - the threshold for inclusion is only two themes.

A Quick Word About Fees and Overlap

GRNJ has the same 0.75% expense ratio as GRNY, so it's quite an expensive fund to own. However, in fairness to Tom Lee, he's about as far from a closet-Indexer as you'll find, so arguably, it's worth it to pay for something different, especially if it can deliver outsized gains. I think that's why GRNY has been so successful, so quickly - word has spread that it's outperforming many large-cap growth benchmarks without much influence from the big tech stocks that have powered the market these last five years.

My analysis reveals GRNJ shares the same unique features as GRNY. To illustrate, I compared its overlap with about 250 U.S. small- and mid-cap ETFs and found it averaged only 5%, which is incredibly low. Although subject to change, GRNJ is most similar to the Invesco S&P MidCap Momentum ETF (XMMO), and even in this case, overlap is less than 20%. You can run your own custom overlap analysis here, but I think you'll find GRNJ complements your existing assets well.

Analysis

GRNY Sector Allocations

One potential downside to GRNJ is that it's highly concentrated at the sector level. Currently, it allocates about two-thirds to just Industrials and Technology combined, as summarized below.

  • Industrials: 38.52%
  • Technology: 24.89%
  • Materials: 7.74%
  • Financials: 7.61%
  • Communication Services: 5.79%
  • Consumer Discretionary: 5.74%
  • Energy: 3.87%
  • Health Care: 3.84%
  • Utilities: 1.98%.

GRNJ Peer Group and Fundamental Analysis

Next, I'd like to dive into GRNJ's fundamentals, as I think this will provide some nice insight into what the fund offers compared to other small/mid-cap funds. As comparators, I've chosen the following four ETFs:

  • Vanguard Small-Cap Growth ETF (VBK)
  • Vanguard Mid-Cap Growth ETF (VOT)
  • Janus Henderson Small/Mid Cap Growth ETF (JSMD)
  • Congress SMID Growth ETF (CSMD).

The following table highlights selected fundamental metrics for GRNJ's top 25 holdings, which total 49.15%. Please have a scan of the list and the summary metrics at the bottom for each ETF, and I'll offer my takeaways afterward.

Takeaway #1: High 1.49 Five-Year Portfolio Beta

One standout statistic is GRNJ's 1.49 five-year portfolio beta, which is the weighted average result of its underlying holdings. While not all volatility should be treated negatively (especially in the current environment), a backtest reveals that an equal-weight portfolio of these stocks would have performed just as badly as VBK and VOT in 2022.

That's not to say GRNJ will experience these same losses, since it will have the opportunity to rotate into more defensive companies through short-term themes, including style tilt. However, I simply want to point out that for the time being, GRNJ seems quite risky, and it's unknown how the Granny Shots team will react in risk-off environments.

Takeaway #2: GRNJ Has Huge Momentum Factor Exposure

One thing I appreciate about the Granny Shots strategy is that it leans heavily on the momentum factor. I'm not sure if it's temporary or not, but we see that GRNJ's holdings have seen their share prices increase by 64.07% over the last year, compared to only 29.71% and 22.52% for VBK and VOT, the two broad-based passive growth funds. JSMD is closest, with its holdings gaining 42.88% on a weighted average basis, but still, GRNJ has some of the best momentum features among all the small/mid-cap ETFs I track.

Takeaway #3: GRNJ Trades At A Reasonably Attractive Valuation

GRNJ trades at 23.36x forward earnings, which, except for JSMD, is actually pretty reasonable compared to its peers. The table below highlights several other common valuation metrics, including its 17.47x trailing P/CF ratio and its 3.21x trailing EV/Sales ratio, both of which are on the low end. Overall, GRNJ receives a 3.99/10 Value Score in my ETF Rankings System, and although that score isn't great on its surface, it's solid for the earnings growth that it offers, which is upwards of 20% on a one-year estimated basis.

Takeaway #4: Margins Aren't Great, But Debt Is Low

The fundamental analysis table highlights GRNJ's 8.62% weighted average EBIT margins, which are lower than VBK's and about half of VOT's. Net margins look slightly better, but JSMD and CSMD do much better on the quality factor while keeping in the same size segment - their weighted average market caps are only $7.5B and $13.6B, respectively.

For a more detailed look at quality using only GRNJ's top ten holdings, please consider the table below, which also shows JSMD and CSMD leading on Gross and EBITDA margins, ROA, ROE, ROTC, and Asset Turnover.

However, GRNJ's holdings use substantially less leverage, evidenced by its 0.26 and 0.68 LTD/Total Capital and Debt/Equity ratios, so I can see how it could perform better in higher-rate environments. I should also note that JSMD's higher 29.10% ROE comes from its higher leverage, since that's one of the ratio's three components, along with net margin and asset turnover.

Investment Recommendation

GRNJ doesn't have any track record yet, so I'm staying neutral for this initial coverage. However, I can confidently say it offers excellent momentum factor exposure not commonly found in the small/mid-cap size segment, and its components don't appear to be excessively overpriced, especially when factoring in the high earnings growth they offer. GRNJ's holdings also have low leverage on average, which might help offset some of the risks of its concentrated high-beta strategy. Still, with quarterly rebalances, there will be enough opportunities to adjust based on market conditions.

In terms of competition, GRNJ could prove to be a nice alternative to VBK and VOT, two broad-based small- and mid-cap growth funds. However, I'll be watching to see if GRNJ can outperform JSMD and CSMD when momentum falls out of favor, as they appear to have stronger factor mixes at the moment. If it can, I think that will be a testament to the Granny Shots strategy, and would likely lead to a Buy rating sometime in 2026.

I hope you enjoyed this "first look" at GRNJ, and as always, I look forward to any questions you might have in the comments below. Thank you for reading.

data.title: Firan Technology Is A Speculative Bet On Defense And Airbus' Growth
data.description:
Firan Technology Group Corporation (OTCPK:FTGFF) is an aerospace electronics supplier of defense and commercial aviation. FTGFF operates ten facilities across North America and Asia, with a new site under development in India. FTGFF focuses on advanced manufacturing rather than general consumer electronics. Concretely, this means they manufacture printed circuit boards (PCBs), with high-density interconnect (HDI) and semi-additive process (SAP). This way, its products can capture the rising demand for lighter, more complex avionics systems. Recently, FTGFF also acquired FLYHT Aerospace, which expanded its data-connectivity solutions that help it provide content for Airbus (OTCPK:EADSF). And, as a whole, I believe FTGFF is well-positioned to benefit from broader secular tailwinds tied to the US spending on defense applications.

Aviation Electronics

Firan Technology Group Corporation is a high-technology manufacturing company that produces aerospace and defense electronics. It was founded back in 1983 under the name Helix Circuit and later renamed itself as Circuit World Corporation. After a reverse takeover in 2003, the company became Firan Technology Group. FTGFF is currently headquartered in Toronto, Ontario, and operates ten manufacturing facilities worldwide. Its footprint includes sites in Canada, the US, China, plus a new upcoming location under construction in India.

As a quick overview, FTGFF has two operating segments: 1) FTG Circuits and 2) FTG Aerospace. First, FTG Circuits produces high-technology printed circuit boards (PCBs) for high-reliability systems, not general consumer electronics. Its PCBs are high-density interconnect (HDI), RF circuitry, thermal management boards, rigid and flex assembly PCBs, and semi-additive process PCBs. This segment serves aviation, defense, space, and high-tech industries, and uses FTGFF's production sites in the US (California, Massachusetts, Minnesota, and Virginia), Canada (Toronto), and China (Tianjin).

As for its other segment, FTG Aerospace develops high-reliability avionics hardware for human-machine interfaces (HMI). For example, FTGFF's HMIs are used in back-lit control panels and assemblies, keyboards, multipurpose control and display units (MCDUs), cursor control devices, bezels, and line-replaceable units (LRUs). This division has manufacturing facilities in the US (California), Canada (Toronto), and China (Tianjin).

With that in mind, FTGFF is now investing in new technologies, such as HDI and SAP PCB processes. Essentially, they want to improve their technology and deliver more circuitry in a smaller area. HDI features a much higher wiring density per unit area compared to standard PCBs. This is why HDIs are typically used in applications where space, weight, and signal integrity are critical, even in harsh environments. SAP is a PCB manufacturing technique that enables ultra-fine circuit patterns using narrower traces and tighter spacing. So, by furthering its HDI and SAP processes, FTGFF should be able to increasingly improve its value proposition.

It's also worth mentioning that last December, FTGFF acquired FLYHT in a transaction valued at $23 million. At the time of the acquisition, FLYHT represented a $20 million increase in annualized sales for FTGFF. Additionally, FLYHT expanded FTGFF's footprint into commercial aerospace aftermarket systems, which is a high-margin niche. This M&A transaction also strengthened FTGFF's ties to Airbus because it provides content for its Airbus aircraft.

Besides, acquiring FLYHT gave FTGFF new technology lines for backup aircraft communication and telemetry systems. This know-how is critical as it's used on all Airbus aircraft (Satcom via Iridium Satellite), a 5G-enabled data recorder transmitting flight and maintenance data, and an aircraft-mounted sensor transmitting high-altitude atmospheric data.

Valuation And Risk Analysis

Now, from a valuation perspective, FTGFF trades at a $190.1 million market cap. Its most recent financials from Q3 2025 show its balance sheet holds only $7.1 million in cash and equivalents. They also have approximately $14.0 million in financial debt (aside from their regular operating liabilities). So, I estimate FTGFF's EV at roughly $197.0 million. And according to Seeking Alpha's dashboard on FTGFF, they're projected to generate $149.7 million in revenues by 2026. This suggests a reasonably cheap forward EV/S of 1.3, which is lower than its sector's median forward EV/S of 3.4.

I also think FTGFF is a reasonably profitable operation. They generated $4.0 million in operating income during their last quarter, implying an annualized EBIT of around $16.0 million. This would put FTGFF at an EV/EBIT ratio of 12.3, which again is considerably cheaper than its peers' EV/EBIT of 25.4. Lastly, I calculate that FTGFF generated roughly $4.0 million in cash flow during Q3 2025. Note that I got this figure by simply adding its cash flows from operations and CAPEX, which suggests this should be a self-sustainable company at this point.

Furthermore, looking forward, FTGFF has a few growth tailwinds. First, management noted that demand in the aerospace and defense sectors should continue increasing in the coming years, in particular with the development of new programs with next-generation aircraft and defense systems. FTGFF's investor presentation also mentioned they intent to continue acquiring new technology (like they did with FLYHT). Plus, FTGFF intends to maximize its capacity across its facilities in Canada, the US, China, and now India while adding inorganic growth through targeted new acquisitions.

It's also worth mentioning that FTGFF is tightly linked to the two dominant aerospace behemoths, Airbus and Boeing (BA). FTGFF supplies components to OEMs like these. So, if their business grows, then FTGFF's revenues are also likely to increase. In that sense, Airbus and Boeing both have a favorable demand outlook. FTGFF's projects that post-pandemic (2020 to 2021) deliveries are starting to recover. Airbus outperforms Boeing and should keep its lead through 2026. Airbus has a larger order pipeline with 8,716 aircraft compared to 6,531 orders from Boeing. However, Boeing will rebound in 2025, with shipments projected to be 50% higher than in 2024.

Similarly, the broader US defense sector is an even larger secular tailwind for FTGFF. In fact, the US has consistently increased its defense spending in recent years, and its 2026 budget request is now close to $1 trillion. And ultimately, higher defense budgets translate to more aircraft and avionics upgrades, and electronics procurement, which are all core areas for FTGFF. Hence, all in all, it's difficult to be bearish on FTGFF, especially since it trades at a very compelling valuation with promising growth prospects.

Naturally, please keep in mind this is a microcap with thin trading liquidity. Its average trading volume is roughly 2,216 shares on the Nasdaq (NDAQ), which at the current $7.50 PPS indicates only $16.6 thousand in trading liquidity. So, ideally, I would avoid buying the OTC shares available in the US because even tiny buy/sell orders can lead to significant slippage. But even its consolidated trading volume is low at 37.3 thousand shares, suggesting a combined trading liquidity (across all exchanges) of just $279.8 thousand. Thus, given its microcap status and thin liquidity, I think you should expect substantial volatility with this stock.

Conclusion: Worth Speculating

Overall, I think FTGFF is a relatively safe long-term bet in the aerospace defense sector. In theory, it should benefit from the increasing US defense spending through its clients like Airbus, which are large defense OEMs. Its valuation also looks compelling, and it has secular tailwinds that I believe will last for the foreseeable future, given the rising geopolitical tensions worldwide. Having said that, FTGFF remains a microcap with thin liquidity, so it's undoubtedly a much more speculative bet. It's also a somewhat small player in its niche, making it a bit riskier than other, much larger component suppliers. Yet, on balance, I still feel a speculative "Buy" is fair at these levels for long-term investors. Just make sure you size your position accordingly.

data.title: AMG GW&K Municipal Bond Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Performance Drivers

  • ​GW&K’s Municipal Bond Fund slightly underperformed the Bloomberg 10-Year Municipal Bond Index for the quarter.
  • ​Municipal bonds posted strong returns in the third quarter, led by September’s sharp move at the long end. July and August returns were dominated by curve steepening rather than broad price gains, with retail demand pinned to the front end while value built further out the curve.
  • ​Broader rates also fell over the quarter, though only by single digits. A weaker labor picture gave the Federal Reserve Board (the Fed) room to cut by 25 basis points in September, but an upgraded outlook for growth and inflation kept a more aggressive rally in check.
  • ​Heading into the fourth quarter, markets are pricing in a slightly more aggressive easing path than the Fed’s own projections, but a range of crosscurrents will determine how that plays out.

Top Contributors/Detractors

  • ​We continued a year-long trend of heavy trading activity in the third quarter as we looked to take advantage of the sharp steepening of the municipal bond yield curve.

Contributors

  • ​An overweight to bonds longer than the Index was the biggest positive as rates declined.

Detractors

  • ​An allocation to shorter maturities than the Index detracted from performance.

Positioning Update

  • ​On the sell side, we focused on short-callable bonds with limited upside, particularly high-quality names that had run their course.
  • ​We redeployed mostly in the 10–13-year area of the curve where the yield and roll created some of the most compelling expected return profiles in over a decade.
  • ​The extension trades provided roughly 100 basis points of nominal yield pickup, with roll dynamics adding another 100-150 basis points.

Outlook

  • ​Municipal bonds enter the fourth quarter in excellent shape. Nominal yields remain well above their 10- and 20-year averages, and the curve is still steep enough to offer meaningful expected return from roll.
  • ​Ratios at the front end are stretched, leaving less cushion if broader yields back up, but value persists in intermediate and longer maturities, where roll and carry do more of the work.
  • ​Credit fundamentals remain sound. States continue to budget conservatively and maintain elevated reserves, even as some have begun modest draws on rainy-day funds.

data.title: Woodward Q4 Earnings Preview: FY26 Outlook Signals More Upside Ahead
data.description:
Woodward (NASDAQ:WWD) shares have risen 5.4% since my last report, modestly outperforming the S&P 500. With the company set to report its Q4 2025 earnings on November 24, investor attention is turning toward the FY26 outlook, margin trends, and free cash flow expectations. In this article, I review Wall Street expectations for Q4 2025, assess how estimates have shifted, and update my valuation and price target for Woodward.

Wall Street Expects A Strong Double Digit Growth Quarter For Woodward

For the fourth quarter, analysts are expecting revenues of $94.15 million indicating 10.4% growth with estimates ranging between $935 million and $9621.1 million. Over the past six months analyst estimates for the fourth quarter have increased by 2.6% and by 0.6%. The company has a strong track record of meeting and exceeding analyst estimates. Over the past twelve quarters, the company missed expectations just once.

EPS estimates point at 32.5% growth with a consensus of $1.87 with estimates ranging from $1.65 to $1.95. Over the past six months the estimates have been revised upward by 10.8% and by 1.8% over the past three months. Over the past three years, the company missed expectations just once with 10 consecutive quarters of beating analyst estimates. The consensus on EPS indicates 32.5% year-on-year growth.

Riding The Aerospace Recovery Train And Defense Tailwinds

Perhaps more interesting than the fourth quarter is the outlook for 2026 and the years beyond. For 2026, analysts expect 9.4% revenue growth to $3.8 billion and EPS growth of 18.3% to $7.82. In the years after, revenue growth is expected to slow down to 7.7% and 2.9% for 2027 and 2028 with EPS growth of 15.8% and 6.3% respectively. So, timing of production rate increases on commercial airplane programs is going to drive the growth but performance also depends on industrial demand. Volatile demand for on-highway sales in China, which basically represents LNG truck sales provide a risk as the market is more susceptible to macroeconomic outlooks.

In Aerospace, the Airbus A350 provides a growth opportunity but increases in the production rates have been sliding. The company has strengthened its content value on the Airbus A350 program through the acquisition of work for the horizontal stabilizer trim actuation system from Safran. Currently Airbus is building at a challenging rate of six airplanes per month, but the plan is to hike production to 10 and 12 per month eventually. Other growing Airbus programs are the A330neo and A320neo.

The company also has content on Boeing airplanes such as the Boeing 737 MAX for which Woodward provides the thrust reverser systems and the Boeing 787. Both programs are growth drivers for Woodward.

In Defense, the company has exposure with growth opportunities for the Future Long-Range Assault Aircraft and Joint Direct Attack Munition or JDAM, which is a guidance kit that can turn “dumb bombs” into precision guided munitions. In the industrial segment, demand for energy may driven power generator demand.

Woodward Stock Still Has Attractive Upside Against Aerospace And Defense Valuation

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

For Woodward, I am increasing my price target from $283.26 to $294.72 based on a peer group valuation and FY26 expectations in mind. This indicates 15% upside in the base case and if we would value the stock a year ahead of earnings the fundamentally supported price target would be $337.77. EBITDA for this year has not changed significantly and up by 0.8% next year and 1% in the year after for an increase in the estimated EBITDA generation of 0.6% between FY25 and FY27. Free cash flow expectations came down driven by higher capital expenditures modelled now. For this year, free cash flow generation came down by 4.4%. So, the increase in the estimate was not so much driven by changes to free cash flow or EBITDA estimates, but by the increase in the peer group EV/EBITDA multiple.

If we look at the key metrics, we see that margins are expected to tick up substantially as the operating leverage is expected to substantially improve as key programs such as the Boeing 737 MAX, Boeing 787, Airbus A320neo and Airbus A350 are expected to see higher production rates in the years ahead. This will also increase the cash flow generation substantially partially offset by higher expected capital expenditures to support higher production rates. Looking at the debt balance, we note that the net debt and net debt leverage have come down substantially since 2023 and the company could be in a net cash position by FY2026. So, we are looking at a company with improving operating leverage, a potential flip from net debt to net cash and strong growth drivers in commercial aerospace, defense and power generation.

Conclusion: Woodward Remains Attractive

For many aerospace companies the “easy upside” has materialized and that means that going forward in many cases we do not see huge upside. Woodward is no exception, but I still believe there is upside with commercial aerospace programs ramping up, higher defense demand and data center power demand. The main risk for the company would be the volatile results for on-highway sales in China. The net cash position will allow the company to increase its share repurchase program or deploy the cash for strategic acquisitions to optimize ship set value on programs with growing volumes in the years to come.

data.title: Kinder Morgan Has Growth Potential With A Modest Dividend After Consolidation
data.description:
Shares of Kinder Morgan (KMI) experienced a massive repricing as they exited a period of consolidation and broke out to the upside at the end of 2023. KMI exceeded $30 per share for a brief period and has been trading between $25 and $30 since the beginning of the year. A.I. is no longer a software story, as it's changing the landscape of the energy sector. Training, running larger language models (LLMs) and other A.I. workloads require enormous amounts of electricity running 24/7. KMI is one of the largest energy infrastructure companies in North America, with roughly 66,000 miles of natural gas pipelines and more than 700 Bcf of working gas storage. My investment thesis is simple: KMI's revenue and adjusted EBITDA will continue to grow as they are responsible for transporting roughly 40% of U.S. natural gas production. With the demand for energy increasing, the upstream producers will need to increase production, and that will drive the volume being moved through KMI's system higher.

KMI gets paid primarily for providing capacity to the upstream segment to move its products, which takes commodity pricing risk off of the table. I believe that over the next several years there will be so much infrastructure built that companies such as KMI will see their market caps increase as their infrastructure will become more important than it already is. KMI is already a tollbooth for the energy sector as it collects fees for the transportation of traditional sources of energy, and those tolls come back to the shareholders through a growing quarterly dividend. I believe that KMI will continue to generate capital appreciation and large amounts of dividend income for its shareholders.

Following up on my previous article

At the beginning of July, I wrote an article about KMI discussing why I felt shares would go higher. Since then, shares of KMI have declined by 4.38%, while the S&P 500 has increased by 6.77%. KMI's total return has been 2.28% after the dividend is factored in. I felt that the demand for energy was continuing to grow and that was bullish for KMI as more data centers came online. KMI was building out over $8 billion worth of infrastructure over the next several years, which will be accretive to its cash from operations and adjusted EBITDA. As the Fed continued to lower rates, I felt that KMI would become more attractive as their cost of capital would decline and their dividend yield would be more attractive compared to the risk-free rate of return. I am following up with a new article to discuss why I am still bullish on KMI even after the share price has traded rangebound for the better part of 2025.

KMI delivered a strong Q3

All of the projects that KMI has brought online have supported the increased demand of volume across its system, which has led to larger levels of profitability. KMI generated $628 million in net income, and their adjusted net income increased 16% YoY to $648 million. KMI's adjusted EPS also increased 16% YoY to $0.29. I like what I saw out of Q3, as KMI's adjusted EBITDA increased 6% YoY to $1.99 billion while its revenue grew 12% YoY to $4.15 billion. The top-line growth and EBITDA expansion are a clear indication that KMI is benefitting from higher volumes and steady demand throughout the oil patch. KMI also experienced larger amounts of cash being generated, as its cash flow from operations was $1.4 billion in Q3, which was an increase of 13% YoY. After their allocations toward CapEx initiatives, KMI's free cash flow (FCF) was $621 million, which was an increase of 5% YoY. KMI is on track to deliver enough FCF to fund its large expansion backlog while growing the modest dividend over time.

In Q3, KMI's operational strength was front and center as its natural gas pipelines stole the show. The volume that the natural gas segment did was a clear indication that more energy is, in fact, moving through KMI's system. KMI's transported volumes averaged 47.5 trillion BTU per day, which was an increase of 6% YoY. Sales volume within natural gas climbed to 3.7 trillion BTU per day, which was up 38% YoY. To round out the metrics, the natural gas gathering volumes increased 9% YoY to 4.4 trillion BTU per day. These gains reflect a mix of LNG feedgas flows, power generation demand, and continued strength in critical basins such as the Permian. On the Q3 earnings call, KMI's management team emphasized that the natural gas segment, which accounts for roughly two-thirds of the business, is outperforming its 2025 forecasts, which is an indication of how strong the portfolio has become. People traditionally think of KMI as just a pipeline company, but their terminals segment continues to provide stable, fee-based income. Its liquid capacity was 78.7 million barrels, with utilization in the mid-90% range generating $274 million in EBDA.

I believe that one of the most important aspects in Q3 was the size of KMI's backlog. At the end of Q3, KMI's backlog was $9.3 billion, which remained unchanged from Q2, but there were about $500 million of projects that came online and were replaced by a similar amount of new projects. Roughly 90% of the backlog is natural gas projects, with the remainder in refined products and terminals. The aspect that I am very bullish about is that about 50% of the backlog is tied directly to power generation projects, which will be needed as more data centers come online.

KMI is expecting that outside of the projects geared toward the CO2 segment, the remaining $7.9 billion of backlog to generate a first-full-year Project EBITDA multiple of roughly 5.7x. I found it interesting that KMI sees an opportunity outside of the contracted backlog with over $10 billion in additional potential projects under evaluation. This is where I tend to get very excited about the future, as I am looking at this from a long-term perspective, and KMI is in a dominant position in the gas transportation and storage industry. As they bring more products online to meet demand, they are likely to benefit from economies of scale, increase their margins, and drive larger amounts of profitability from their network of energy infrastructure assets.

KMI is positioned for the future

The combination of Microsoft (MSFT), Amazon (AMZN), Alphabet (GOOGL), and Meta Platforms (META) has allocated $217.27 billion toward CapEx in 2024, and over the trailing twelve months (TTM), this has increased 51.7% ($112.49 billion) to $329.76 billion. Based on their Q3 earnings call, these companies are expected to spend $354.55 billion in CapEx, which is an increase of 63.19%, or $137.28 billion YoY. These companies are spending aggressively on data centers, and none of them have indicated that they will be slowing down anytime soon. MSFT, AMZN, and GOOGL have over $700 billion in backlog for cloud services, and there isn't enough compute to meet demand. As more data centers come online, they will need a massive amount of power, and KMI is positioned well to help facilitate the additional demand coming to market.

I believe that KMI has positioned itself as a critical backbone of the A.I. era as it's moving the fuel necessary to power the data centers and industrial facilities. KMI is connected to all major producing basins and key demand centers with access to the Gulf Coast for exporting. KMI's management estimates that about half of the current backlog supports power generation, and they are actively pursuing more than 10 Bcf/d of additional natural gas opportunities for power plants. Many of the opportunities that KMI has in front of them are tied to regions seeing heavy data center construction, manufacturing reshoring, and population growth. The scale of these projects is comparable to when the backlog was only about $3 billion, yet the current backlog is more than three times that size. This is an indication that KMI is still in the early innings of monetizing this new wave of demand.

KMI has some fundamental growth drivers, including LNG exports and surging electricity demand, which could help its adjusted EBITDA expand. From an LNG perspective, KMI has long-term contracts to move almost 8 Bcf/d of natural gas to LNG export facilities. As projects now under construction come online, that contracted volume is expected to rise to almost 12 Bcf/d by the end of 2028. KMI's management believes that capacity for U.S. LNG could more than double by 2030, which is bullish for KMI, as their pipes and storage are directly connected to many of those facilities. I believe that as LNG exports continue to ramp up and A.I.-driven power demand expands, KMI's backlog being put in place will add a large amount of capacity that could become very profitable for shareholders.

Risks to investing in KMI

While I am bullish on KMI, there are several risk factors to consider. Investors should do their own due diligence before allocating capital toward KMI or any other investments. KMI's future coincides with the CapEx expansion for data centers to a degree because if the hyperscalers decide to pull back on their CapEx allocation, KMI's backlog of projects could be reduced. If that occurs, it could change their forecast for future profitability. If the Fed decides to keep rates at these levels and if they end up taking rates higher, it would cripple KMI, as their cost of capital would increase, which would put some future projects in jeopardy and increase their interest expenses. While KMI is one of the largest energy infrastructure plays, they have $39 billion in long-term debt, and the Fed could create an environment that is more difficult for them.

Conclusion

I believe that the demand for energy is going to expand over the next several years, which will cause the upstream producers to increase their drilling capacity and move larger amounts of traditional energy sources. KMI should be a direct beneficiary, as natural gas will play a critical role in the development of data centers. As the Fed continues to cut rates, KMI's dividend, which is yielding 4.31%, will look more attractive as the risk-free rate of return declines. KMI is one of the premier energy infrastructure companies in the country, and I think it will get re-rated higher as the demand for energy increases and the story starts to shift from data center plays to the companies that are benefiting from the expansion. The big difference between KMI and the data center plays is that once these companies stop spending increased amounts of CapEx to build the data centers, they will still need continuous energy to keep the data centers running, and KMI will benefit for decades to come. I think KMI is an opportunity for both appreciation and dividend income.

data.title: Wix: Base44 Is A Knockout, But There's A Cost (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Wix.com Ltd. (WIX) shares are selling off in pre-market trading, down over 7% despite beating top and bottom line estimates, and raising guidance. This reflects a continuation of the trend we've seen in recent quarters.

Wix's revenue growth and stock price are moving in opposite directions, as the market continues to fear AI disruption.

While Wix owns one of the fastest-growing AI-powered vibe coding platforms in Base44, this is coming at a meaningful cost, with margins deteriorating already in Q3 and set to drop further.

To me, Wix's growth story remains strong, but previous growth-over-profit periods have led to poor shareholder returns. The question investors should ask is whether this time is different.

I think it isn't, and downgrade the stock to Hold.

Wix's Rise Was Short-Lived, As Usual

With a stock like Wix, investors should never get too comfortable. I published my initial article on the company in April 2025, saying it's a Strong Buy given significant undervaluation and growth catalysts. Back then, the stock was at $150 per share.

At one point, the stock surpassed $180, but it's now all the way back to below $120, based on the pre-market price.

Those rollercoaster-like movements shouldn't be anything new for long-time Wix followers, and frankly, considering the company's narrow moat, they also make sense.

At the end of the day, Wix has many competitors. It's mainly reliant on marketing (rather than brand or direct relationship) for customer acquisition. Its management has a history of willingness to sacrifice profits. And, the company is now facing the threat of AI, as it seems every day a different model company launches a great web-building tool.

As a result, for Wix to deliver outsized growth, it typically needs to sacrifice margins, and vice versa. And, it's very hard for a company to manage the right balance between those, amid volatile investor preferences.

Wix Delivers Strong Growth, But At A Price

Revenue in Q3 was $505 million, up 14% Y/Y, accelerating from the 12% mark in Q2. Business Solutions reached a record $149 million, while Creative Subscriptions reached $356 million, growing 18% and 12%, respectively.

Total registered users are at 299 million, an all-time high and a strong leading indicator for future growth. Meanwhile, two of the key growth engines are driving strong momentum, with Partners (professional designers) revenue up 24% Y/Y, and Transactions revenue up 20%, as Wix's focus on GPV growth is paying off, with GPV reaching $3.7 billion in the quarter, up 13%.

Wix certainly delivered strong growth metrics (we'll get to Base44 later), but as usual, it comes at a cost. Gross margin dropped by 1 point Q/Q, and operating expenses as a percentage of revenue jumped 4 points. This translates to a return to operating loss for the first time since 2023.

This level of aggressiveness, for me, is making Wix borderline uninvestable. I mean, how could I invest in a company that's moving from profit to loss on a dime? It's also putting a big question mark about the growth achieved in the quarter.

The Base 44 Knockout Pressures The Profitability Outlook

Base44 is on track to deliver at least $50 million ARR by the end of 2025, and their next goal is $100 million. It now serves 2 million users, up 7x from June, and has surpassed 10% traffic share among vibe coding websites.

Wix believes that the app development market is far larger than website building, which means Base44 more than doubles its TAM.

My issue is that many companies can "buy" their way to growth. This does not create shareholder value, and doesn't mean growth will last if and when the marketing dollars stop.

We're seeing the magnitude of Wix's aggressive marketing push by the company turning to a loss, and the question is whether this spend will end up generating a positive ROI.

Wix is showcasing the above graph show that their historical return on marketing turns positive after three quarters. And, because we're in the first quarter of the current aggressive push, the impact on profit is most severe. Still, I'm skeptical about how sustainable this growth is without marketing, given the intense competition, and I'm also worried about the length of this aggressive marketing cycle.

While Wix slightly raised its revenue target for the year, it also cut the profit guidance and narrowed its FCF target lower, reflecting their current priorities.

Valuation

At $118 per share, which is the pre-market price at the time of writing, Wix's market cap is estimated at about $6.6 billion. On an FCF outlook of $600 million, we're talking about a P/FCF multiple of about 11, which is considered low, especially for companies growing at a double-digit pace.

However, roughly 40% of Wix's free cash flow is eaten by stock-based comp. Adjusting for it, we're talking about a multiple above 18x, not as attractive.

Meanwhile, Wix is trading at around 18 times forward earnings, which, again, seems more attractive than it is given SBC. Also, it's unclear whether earnings will grow next year, given the outsized spending outlook.

Conclusion

While Wix's valuation seems low, it's hard to gauge the future outlook for the company's profitability. When accounting for stock-based compensation, or SBC, I think valuation reasonably reflects Wix's narrower moat, and concerns about near-term growth & profit balance.

Base44 has been a knockout in terms of growth, but it seems to have completely changed the company's focus and profitable growth trajectory, which was the basis of my previous bullishness.

Until we get further clarity about the ROI on this marketing spend, I downgrade Wix to Hold.

data.title: Carillon Reams Core Plus Bond Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

Tariff uncertainty lingers

During the third quarter, a range of tariffs were implemented, accompanied by the announcement of additional levies, even as bilateral trade agreements with the United Kingdom and Japan were enacted. The complexity and volume of these measures make it difficult to assess their implications. Moreover, the economic impact remains uncertain, particularly following a federal appeals court ruling that deemed many of the tariffs unlawful. While a U.S. Supreme Court decision is anticipated in the fourth quarter, the contested tariffs were enforced, contributing to heightened ambiguity in the broader economic outlook.

Labor market weakness

Nonfarm payroll data released earlier in the year pointed to a moderating labor market. However, August revisions removed nearly 260,000 jobs from the prior two months’ totals, significantly altering that initial assessment. The annual benchmark update released in September introduced a larger than anticipated downward revision, further weakening the outlook for this component of the U.S. Federal Reserve’s (Fed) dual mandate. Meanwhile, a contracted labor pool, driven by a decline in foreign-born workers, helped to keep the unemployment rate low. This divergence from payroll data suggests the rate may be artificially suppressed, complicating the Fed’s evaluation of labor market conditions.

Inflation remains above target

Services inflation has remained persistently elevated, posing a significant obstacle to achieving the Federal Reserve’s 2% inflation target. While goods inflation has yet to reflect the impact of tariffs implemented in August, the potential for temporary price increases, should the tariffs remain in effect, suggests that further progress toward the target may be limited. Despite the challenges in bringing short-term inflation back in line, long-term inflation expectations remain well-anchored, indicating continued market confidence in the Fed’s commitment to price stability.

Fed makes ‘insurance cut’ to rates and maintains independence

At its September meeting, the Federal Reserve approved a widely anticipated 25-basis point rate cut, which Fed Chair Jerome Powell characterized as an “insurance cut.” The move reflects growing concern within the Fed over signs of labor market softening, prompting a preference for easing monetary conditions despite inflation remaining above the 2% target and ahead of any potential inflationary effects from recently implemented tariffs. Meanwhile, ongoing pressure from President Trump was largely disregarded by the committee, as the Fed’s institutional framework continues to insulate its policy decisions from direct executive influence.

Spread sectors were positive during the third quarter

High yield (HY) credit led the way again in the third quarter as a “risk-on” mood was evident through much of the quarter. Investment grade (IG) credit, mortgage-backed securities (MBS), commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS), and asset-backed securities (ABS) all had positive excess returns. MBS excess returns were notable as they lagged the credit market most of the year but kept pace in the third quarter. The yield curve steepened as the Fed’s September rate cut and expectations of another cut in October pushed the front end of the curve lower. The long end moved modestly lower as long-term inflation expectations remained anchored. The curve is now inverted out to the 2-year point but is steeper in slope beyond this point.

Characteristics

Total Net Assets (billions): $2.01

Number of holdings: 300

Top 10 Holdings

U.S. Treasury N/B T 4 1/4 08/15/54

FNCL 5.5 10/23

U.S. Treasury N/B T 4 1/4 08/15/35

U.S. Treasury N/B T 3 7/8 08/31/32

U.S. Treasury N/B T 3 7/8 08/15/34

TSY INFL IX N/B TII 2 3/8 02/15/55

FNCL 5 10/23

FNCL 4 10/19

FNCL 4.5 10/19

Treasury Bill B 12/09/25

Portfolio Review

Rate volatility decreased during the third quarter of 2025, with the ICE BofAML MOVE Index declining to levels not seen since 2022.

Compared to the beginning of the quarter, the entire yield curve shifted downward with a more pronounced decline at the short end. This led to a steepening of the curve. The Fund’s longer duration positioning benefited performance during the quarter with yield curve positioning also contributing positively.

Sector allocation contributed to performance during the quarter with all spread sectors generating performance relative to the benchmark. The largest contributor during the quarter was mortgage-backed securities, which saw sharp spread tightening during September. Selection within mortgages was a positive contributor as lower-coupon mortgages outperformed during the quarter.

Structured macro trades provided a tailwind as tactical trades benefiting from declining levels of volatility across the globe were implemented. An overweight to investment grade corporates was additive as spreads tightened throughout the quarter, nearing the tightest levels of recent history. Similarly, the carry associated with the high yield position contributed as spreads in that asset also continued to tighten. Security selection within investment grade was a contributor, offsetting its negative effect during the second quarter and bringing selection to a positive contribution year to date. The overweight to asset-backed securities (ABS) positively contributed to performance as selection remained a key focus for the team.

CMBS remained a small positive for the Fund. The main relative detractor was the underweight allocation to government-related securities, which saw positive performance relative to the benchmark but which remained less attractive than other spread sectors. Emerging-market foreign exchange was a slight detractor on a relative basis.

The allocation to agency mortgage-backed securities decreased notably during the quarter as the Fund took profits following a sharp tightening of spreads in September. Agency MBS ended the quarter below benchmark weight, though non-agency and agency combined constituted an overweight. The allocation to IG corporates decreased, largely through a reduction in industrials exposure, which moved to underweight relative to the index. The high yield allocation decreased as opportunities presented themselves to take profits throughout the quarter but remained at a level above the start of the year. ABS exposure was the largest quarter-over-quarter increase as the opportunity set became more attractive, though it should be noted the exposure was all AAA with no subprime. The weight in CMBS remained flat and continued to reside at a low absolute exposure as this asset class continues to require selectivity. The exposure to non-U.S. dollar bonds remained and the allocation to non-U.S. dollar currencies increased, ending the quarter with a small net non-U.S. exposure. The weight in U.S. Treasurys increased during the quarter.

Portfolio duration increased both in absolute and benchmark-relative terms during the quarter as the yield curve steepened. Duration grew to slightly above neutral range, reflecting real rates that are attractive on a long-term basis but acknowledging the possible contribution to the volatility of rates given current market pricing. Yield curve exposure at the end of the quarter featured overweights to the 0- to 1- year, 2- to 5-year, 5- to 7-year, and 7- to 10-year segments. This was offset by underweights to the 1- to 2- year, 10- to 20-year and 20- to 30-year segments.

Outlook

Tariff impact unknown, but legal ruling anticipated in Q4

The U.S. Supreme Court has agreed to hear arguments concerning the Trump administration’s authority to impose tariffs, following an appellate court ruling that found the action unlawful. Should the court uphold the tariffs, the economic implications, particularly for inflation and consumer demand, are likely to unfold gradually, as broad-based levies typically do not produce immediate effects.

Conversely, if the appellate court’s decision is affirmed, uncertainty will persist. Rebating previously collected tariffs could prove administratively complex, and the fiscal impact may be significant, given that tariff revenues were already allocated to fund enacted spending. Moreover, the administration is expected to explore alternative legal avenues to maintain tariff measures, leaving the ultimate economic impact unresolved.

Fed dot plot points to more cuts

Although the Federal Reserve cites labor market weakness as its primary rationale for easing, financial conditions appeared accommodative even prior to the September rate cut. Inflation risks persist, with current measures remaining above the Fed’s 2% target, raising questions about the timing and justification for additional monetary accommodation. Should the anticipated rate cuts fail to materialize, elevated capital market valuations could face increased vulnerability. There is also growing concern that the Fed may be inadvertently easing into a stagflationary environment, where stimulus proves ineffective in spurring growth while exacerbating inflation that is already running above target, potentially resulting in a policy misstep.

Developed market debts grow unabated

Fitch Ratings downgraded France’s credit rating to A+ during the third quarter, following Moody’s downgrade of the United States in May. Both actions underscored growing concerns over fiscal discipline among developed economies. Persistent, unfunded spending continues to strain the fiscal health of nations still grappling with post-pandemic budgetary imbalances. As global interest rates have risen over the past several years, debt-servicing costs have increasingly crowded out other key budget priorities. While concerns about global investors potentially shunning U.S. Treasurys due to fiscal profligacy remain, similar fiscal deterioration across other developed markets has tempered the relative impact of such risks.

‘Everything is going to be OK’ is a crowded trade

Current market valuations offer limited compensation for what we view as considerable risks to the prevailing “Goldilocks” narrative.

Investor sentiment continues to trend toward optimism, with growing expectations that economic and market conditions will remain favorable despite a narrowing probability that key risks can be fully avoided. In this environment, investors may be better served by preparing for an increasingly likely setback rather than by relying on the assumption that a low-probability, benign outcome will materialize.
